topic: Part 5: Kill-Shot Binary Tests — full section rewrite (EXP-057 integration)

queue_item.reason: EXP-057 integrated — Part 5 Kill-Shot HTML fully rewritten with verdict table, strength ratings, per-test analysis

prior_review: SEC-5.1.c2.json (2026-04-09T18:45:00Z) — reviewed the pre-rewrite version at 19,430 chars. Current version is 24,111 chars (+24% growth). Verdict table and strength ratings are new. Several prior-review recommendations remain unaddressed.
our_argument_summary: Part 5 presents six kill-shot tests from the dome's own kill-shot page under the dome's binary rule: 'if any single test confirms, globe is falsified; if any single test fails, dome is falsified.' Our section assigns verdicts (Misleading, Not Demonstrated, Std Model Explains ×3, Refuted by Data), strength ratings (Decisive/Major/Supportive), and detailed per-test rebuttals. The new verdict summary table makes the six verdicts scannable at a glance. Test 6 (NMP drift) is the strongest — failing at 39.9% on the dome's own tracker, which by the dome's binary rule should falsify the model.

holes_found.description: NMP error figure math discrepancy. Our text says the dome predicts '~22.8 km/yr for 2026' and observed drift is 'approximately 35 km/yr.' But |22.8−35|/35 = 34.8%, not 39.9%. We cite '39.9% error' directly from the dome's dashboard without verifying it. The 39.9% figure requires either (a) observed ~38 km/yr (not ~35), or (b) a different denominator convention (e.g., |pred−obs|/pred = 53.4%). Our text uncritically adopts the dome's self-reported error while simultaneously accusing the dome of numerical sleight-of-hand. If a dome defender checks our arithmetic, we look incompetent at exactly the moment we're calling THEM out for bad math. This is the most exploitable single sentence in the section.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) compute the error independently — state the observed rate from WMM2025/IGRF-14, compute |pred−obs|/obs, and report OUR number alongside the dome's claimed 39.9%; or (b) explicitly note: 'The dome's dashboard reports 39.9% error; this implies an observed rate closer to 38 km/yr rather than the ~35 km/yr reported in Livermore et al. (2020). Either the dome uses a different observed value, or the error calculation uses a non-standard denominator. In either case, the prediction fails the dome's own 30% falsification threshold.' This turns the discrepancy into a data point rather than a vulnerability.

holes_found.description: Verdict table 'Dome Says' column misrepresents the dome for Test 6. The column header is 'Dome Says' — reader expects the dome's own characterization. For Test 6, it reads 'Failing' (with ks-failing class). But the dome's live dashboard explicitly marks NMP drift rate as 'PASS (39.9% error).' The dome does NOT say it's failing — the dome says PASS. Putting our editorial judgment in a column labelled 'Dome Says' is exactly the kind of mischaracterization we criticize the dome for. A defender will immediately screenshot this as evidence our review is dishonest.
holes_found.recommendation: Change the Test 6 'Dome Says' cell to 'PASS (39.9%)' (or similar). Move 'Failing' to the 'Our Verdict' or 'Key Issue' column. Alternatively, rename the column to 'Status' and apply editorial annotations there. The current labeling is a credibility trap.

holes_found.description: Opening paragraph inconsistency with summary table. Text: 'Two are claimed as confirmed; four are pending.' Table: 2 Confirmed + 3 Pending + 1 Failing = 6. If the dome's kill-shot page says all four unconfirmed tests are 'pending,' then the opening text is accurate but the table contradicts it (showing only 3 pending). If the dome's dashboard calls NMP 'PASS,' then neither 'pending' nor 'failing' is what the dome says. The reader sees the opening claim of '4 pending' and then a table showing only 3 pending — they'll wonder which is correct.
holes_found.recommendation: Align opening text with table. If the dome's kill-shot page lists 4 pending, say so and note in the table that the live dashboard contradicts this by marking NMP as PASS. If the dome says PASS, the opening text should say 'Two claimed confirmed, three pending, and one marked PASS despite 39.9% error.'

holes_found.description: Test 5 (Eclipse 2026) remains a single paragraph delegating entirely to Section 4.2. The prior review (SEC-5.1.c2.json) specifically flagged this as the 'highest-priority structural fix' and a skim-path vulnerability. The rewrite did not address this. A motivated reader who opens the Kill-Shot tab and finds five detailed multi-paragraph rebuttals plus one 4-sentence stub will conclude we don't have a real rebuttal for the eclipse — the one test that's actually upcoming and falsifiable. This is the most dangerous reader-path weakness in the section.
holes_found.recommendation: Expand Test 5 to at least three paragraphs inline: (1) the dome's specific prediction (−17 to −21 nT at Ebro; dual-baseline spanning −5 to −26 nT across 9 stations); (2) why the Chapman ionospheric mechanism (1933, peer-reviewed for 90+ years) already predicts 5–25 nT under the same conditions, making the test non-discriminating; (3) the three inconsistent prediction numbers documented in Section 4.2; (4) the geographic-path dependency on Besselian ephemerides. Keep the 'see Section 4.2 for full analysis' link, but the standalone tab reader must get the core argument without leaving the page.

holes_found.description: Polaris polar offset value inconsistency persists (flagged in prior review, still unresolved). Test 2 states 'offset by 0.66–0.74° (currently ~0.66° and precessing).' WIN-065 detail_evidence gives 'declination ~89.26°' → offset 0.74°, which it presents as the current value. The actual 2026 offset is ~0.63° (declination ~89.37°). Our section and WIN-065 give different 'current' values for the same quantity, and both are slightly wrong for 2026. A dome defender reading both will quote both and ask which of our two contradictory values is correct.
holes_found.recommendation: Reconcile: use ~0.63° as the 2026 value (from Hipparcos proper motion extrapolation or USNO tables), update both Test 2 and WIN-065 simultaneously, and cite the source. Recommended phrasing: 'Polaris is currently ~0.63° from the north celestial pole (2026 epoch; declination ~89.37°; shrinking toward ~0.46° minimum near 2102).'

holes_found.description: Aubert (2015) citation misapplication in Test 4 persists (flagged in prior review). Aubert forecasts a global surface field decrease (1.46 ± 0.4 μT by 2065), not a localized African SAA cell value for 2026–2028. We cite it to support the claim that mainstream models predict SAA weakening, but the citation doesn't actually predict the localized cell value the dome is targeting. A defender will say: 'Your citation is about a global trend over 40 years — show me a mainstream model that specifically predicts my African cell 21,500 nT threshold.'
holes_found.recommendation: Replace or reframe: either cite IGRF-14 forward extrapolation for the African cell specifically (which would show continued weakening consistent with the dome's extrapolation), or soften the framing to 'Aubert (2015) forecasts broad surface-field weakening consistent with the observed SAA trend, but the dome's specific threshold prediction is derived from the same CHAOS-7 station data that mainstream models use — making it non-discriminating regardless of the exact value.'

holes_found.description: No closing paragraph invoking the dome's own binary rule. The prior review specifically recommended: 'By the dome's own binary rule, a single failing test falsifies the model. Test 6 is currently failing... the dome should be considered falsified by its own scoring criteria.' This is the rhetorical capstone of the entire section — the dome wrote the rule, and the dome's own data triggers it. Currently the section ends abruptly after Test 6's detailed analysis. The punchline is missing.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a closing paragraph after Test 6: 'Summary: The dome's binary rule states that a single failing test falsifies the model. Test 6 (NMP drift rate) currently shows 39.9% error on the dome's own dashboard — exceeding even the dome's self-set 30% falsification threshold before tolerance widening. Of the six tests: one is failing by the dome's own numerics, two 'confirmations' are calibration artifacts or within measurement noise, and three pending tests are non-discriminating because standard physics predicts the same outcomes. By the dome's own rules, the kill-shot framework currently favors dome falsification, not globe falsification.'

holes_found.description: No explicit discussion of the 'Wait and See' verdict category. ISS-678 (which drove this rewrite via EXP-057) specifically defined a new 'Wait and See' verdict for genuinely prospective, non-trivial, falsifiable dome predictions. The Eclipse 2026 test (Test 5) is arguably the best candidate — the eclipse hasn't happened, the dome has registered a specific numerical prediction, and the observation will occur in August 2026. Our verdict of 'Std Model Explains' pre-judges the outcome. Even if Chapman predicts the same range, there IS a genuinely prospective test here: what if the dome's Ebro prediction is outside Chapman's range? A fair reviewer should acknowledge this.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a note to Test 5 explaining why 'Wait and See' was considered but not applied (e.g., 'The eclipse test is genuinely pending, but because the Chapman mechanism already predicts the same range, the test is non-discriminating regardless of outcome — confirmation of −17 to −21 nT would support both models equally'), or (b) actually apply 'Wait and See' to the eclipse test with a conditional note: 'If the observed anomaly falls within 5–25 nT, the test is non-discriminating. If it falls significantly outside that range in the dome's predicted direction, the dome model gains a genuine prospective confirmation. We will update this verdict after August 12, 2026.' The latter is more epistemically honest and demonstrates that our review isn't just reflexively hostile.

holes_found.description: Test 1 strength rating (⭐⭐ Major) may be too low. Test 1 contains the MDS-vs-Finsler self-disagreement (460 km on the same city pair), the calibration-not-prediction argument, the railway-vs-geodesic mismatch, and the V12→V13 parameter inflation. This is at least as strong as Test 3 (⭐⭐⭐ Decisive), which relies on seasonal/latitudinal variation data. The self-disagreement alone (two methods within the dome's own model producing different answers for the same pair) is arguably a self-contradiction, which is our strongest verdict category. Rating it below Test 3 could look like motivated reasoning — downrating the test where the dome has a 'confirmed' result.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider upgrading Test 1 to ⭐⭐⭐ Decisive, or at minimum adding a note explaining why it isn't: e.g., 'Rated Major rather than Decisive because the dome could address the self-disagreement by choosing one method over the other — the calibration argument alone does not falsify, it reclassifies.'

holes_found.description: Test 1 Problem 3 buries the strongest factual claim. The prior review flagged that the MDS-vs-Finsler self-disagreement (460 km gap on Sydney-Perth) is 'the single most rhetorically powerful factual claim in the entire kill-shot section' but is the second-to-last sentence of the last problem's paragraph about Sydney-Buenos Aires. A skim reader will miss it entirely. The rewrite did not promote this to its own labelled problem.
holes_found.recommendation: Create 'Problem 4 — the dome's own coordinate system disagrees with itself' as a separate labelled sub-section, pulling the MDS-vs-Finsler comparison out of the Sydney-Buenos Aires discussion.

holes_found.description: Test 3 'framing problem' paragraph ends with 'The wind IS the explanation' without preempting the dome defender's best counter: 'Then show me a formula that predicts the 55–75 minute asymmetry from first principles without calibration to measured wind speeds.' The honest answer is that both models rely on measured fields — but the jet stream is measured independently (dropsondes, radiosondes, satellite retrievals, ERA5 reanalysis), while 'aetheric slipstream' has no independent measurement. This distinction needs to be stated explicitly.
holes_found.recommendation: Add after the 'wind is the explanation' sentence: 'The jet stream is measured directly and independently of flight times (GPS dropsondes, satellite wind retrievals, ERA5 reanalysis). Flight-time asymmetry is then derived from these independently measured winds. The dome's aetheric slipstream has no independent measurement — it is defined circularly as whatever causes the asymmetry.'

stronger_arguments.description: Test 1: apply the V13 Finsler formula to short, well-measured southern hemisphere routes that have no railway to calibrate against — e.g., Sydney to Auckland (~2,157 km great-circle). If the formula wasn't tested on these routes, or if it produces large errors, that's evidence of selective publication. The absence of reverse tests on non-calibration routes is damning.

Test 6: directly quantify the asymmetric scorecard. The dome marks NMP as PASS at 39.9% error but marks minor globe mismatches as FAIL at <10% error elsewhere on the same dashboard. Quote both thresholds side by side. This transforms the NMP critique from 'they're being generous to themselves' into 'here are the exact numbers showing a double standard.'

The dome's binary rule ('if any single test fails, dome is falsified') is stated on the dome's page but never applied to Test 6. Point out that the dome has not retracted or updated the binary rule after Test 6's failure — the rule is still on the page, still making the falsification commitment. This isn't an interpretation; it's the dome's own words applied to the dome's own data.
sources: Dome kill-shot page (opening statement about binary rule)

kernel_of_truth.description: The binary-test framework is genuinely admirable. Most pseudoscientific models avoid falsifiability entirely — they make vague claims, move goalposts, or refuse to commit to specific numbers. The dome's kill-shot page publishes six tests with specific numerical thresholds and commits in writing that a single failure falsifies the model. This is more epistemic hygiene than most fringe physics offers. The Eclipse 2026 test, in particular, is a genuinely prospective prediction with a specific date and measurable outcome. Credit where due: the author put skin in the game.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Epistemic commitment only counts if honored. Test 6 (NMP) is currently failing by the dome's own numbers, yet the dome marks it PASS via tolerance widening. The binary rule that promised falsifiability has become a one-way ratchet: confirmations are counted immediately, failures are reclassified. A framework that looks falsifiable on paper but is unfalsifiable in practice is worse than no framework — it gives the reader a false sense of rigor.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender would argue: (1) 'Your NMP error math doesn't add up — you say 35 km/yr observed but cite 39.9% error, which implies ~38 km/yr. You can't even check the arithmetic while accusing me of bad math.' (2) 'Test 5 (Eclipse) is a genuinely prospective prediction with a specific date and station — why are you pre-judging it as Std Model Explains before the data exists? That's exactly the kind of prejudgment your evaluation guide says you'll avoid.' (3) 'Your verdict table says I claim Test 6 is Failing — I don't. My dashboard says PASS. You put your words in my mouth in a column labelled Dome Says. That's dishonest.' (4) 'Test 1's verdict of Misleading assumes I calibrated to the Indian Pacific, but my V13 formula is a general-purpose metric — it also produces the Buenos Aires distance to within 8.4%. You can't dismiss both the calibration point AND the non-calibration point.' (5) 'You rate JFK-LHR as Decisive but your seasonal-variation data (62/51 minutes) is from Williams 2016, which is about climate-change impacts on transatlantic flights — not a study of the jet stream itself. Did you actually read the paper?'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Fix the three factual vulnerabilities (NMP math, Dome Says column, Polaris offset) immediately — these are where the defense has the most traction. For the eclipse prejudgment, add the 'Wait and See' discussion per the recommendation above. For the Williams citation, verify the specific 62/51 minute figures against the paper's actual data tables.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: (1) Polaris offset: Test 2 says '0.66–0.74° (currently ~0.66°)' but WIN-065 says declination ~89.26° → 0.74° as 'current.' Actual 2026 value is ~0.63°. (2) NMP drift rate: Test 6 says 'approximately 35 km/yr' but WIN-043 and WIN-059 may cite different observed values — need to cross-check that all three use the same source year. (3) SAA field strength: Test 4 discusses the African cell prediction (PRED-R002) but the same prediction appears in WIN-035 with potentially different framing.

quantitative_verification.computations: Python verification: (1) 55×exp(−0.08×11) = 22.82 km/yr — matches our '~22.8 km/yr' claim. (2) Haversine(Sydney, Perth) = 3,291 km — matches our '3,291 km' and dome's '3,287 km' to within coordinate precision. (3) Polaris declination 89.37° → offset 0.63° for 2026 — does NOT match our '~0.66°' or WIN-065's implied '0.74°'. (4) |22.8−35|/35 = 34.8% — does NOT match the cited '39.9%'. The 39.9% requires observed ~38 km/yr (solved: 22.8/0.601 ≈ 37.9).
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (1) NMP error: our text cites '39.9% error' from the dome dashboard but our own stated values (22.8 predicted, ~35 observed) yield 34.8%. The 39.9% figure is internally consistent on the dome dashboard only if the observed rate is ~38 km/yr, not ~35. We are using the dome's error percentage without verifying it against our own stated inputs. (2) Polaris offset: our '~0.66°' is slightly high for 2026 (~0.63° is more accurate). This was already flagged in the prior review.

deeper_analysis:
The EXP-057 rewrite is a substantial improvement over the pre-rewrite version. The verdict summary table makes the section scannable. The strength ratings provide useful heuristic guidance. The per-test analysis is generally strong and well-sourced. However, five issues from the prior review (SEC-5.1.c2.json) remain unaddressed: (1) Polaris offset value inconsistency with WIN-065, (2) Test 5 still a single paragraph delegating to Section 4.2, (3) Aubert 2015 misapplication in Test 4, (4) MDS-vs-Finsler self-disagreement buried in Test 1 Problem 3, (5) No closing paragraph invoking the dome's binary rule. Two NEW issues emerge from the rewrite: (A) the verdict table's 'Dome Says: Failing' for Test 6 misrepresents the dome's own characterization (they say PASS), and (B) the ISS-678-mandated 'Wait and See' category is not discussed or applied to any test.

The most critical fix is the NMP error math. Our text simultaneously claims ~22.8 km/yr predicted and ~35 km/yr observed, then cites 39.9% error. These three numbers are mutually inconsistent. A dome defender who does the division will catch this instantly, and it will undermine our credibility on the very test where we have the strongest argument. Fix this before anything else.

Second priority: the 'Dome Says' column for Test 6. Putting our editorial judgment in a column labelled with the dome's voice is exactly the kind of mischaracterization we spend 24,000 characters criticizing the dome for. This must be fixed.

Third priority: Test 5 expansion. The Eclipse 2026 is the only genuinely prospective test with a specific upcoming date. Treating it as a 4-sentence afterthought signals to the reader that we either don't take it seriously or don't have a real rebuttal. Both impressions are wrong — we have strong arguments (Chapman, inconsistent numbers, Besselian dependency) — but they're all in Section 4.2, invisible to the kill-shot tab reader.

Overall, the section is 78% of the way to being excellent. The verdict framework is strong. The individual test analyses are detailed and well-sourced (with the caveats above). The improvements needed are surgical — fix three factual errors, expand one paragraph, add one closing paragraph, and the section becomes very hard to attack.

citations_verified: Finlay et al. 2020 DOI 10.1186/s40623-020-01288-x — correct, CHAOS-7 paper in Earth Planets Space
Williams 2016 DOI 10.1088/1748-9326/11/2/024008 — valid DOI, Environmental Research Letters, paper on transatlantic flight times
Livermore et al. 2020 Nature Geoscience — real paper on NMP acceleration via hydromagnetic jet at core surface
Indian Pacific railway 4,352 km — consistent with published sources
Sydney-Perth globe geodesic 3,291 km — verified via Haversine calculation (3,291 km)
Chapman 1933 mechanism and 5-25 nT range — Chapman & Ferraro theory well-established
citations_failed: Aubert 2015 '1.46 ± 0.4 μT surface field decrease by 2065' — number may be correct for global field but is cited as evidence about the localized African SAA cell (carried over from prior review)

Williams 2016 seasonal asymmetry figures '62 min winter, 51 min summer' — paper is primarily about climate-change impacts on jet stream and transatlantic times, specific minute figures need verification against paper's data tables (carried over from prior review)
citations_unchecked: Terra-Nova et al. 2017 PNAS — cited briefly under Test 4 for SAA bifurcation documentation
V12-to-V13 Finsler formula evolution timeline and -78% / -8.4% error figures — sourced from dome's own pages, not independently verified
